context_update.summary: This chunk introduces Fermanal Fe-Mn-Al alloys, the research aim, and experimental procedures. Over 100 heats with Mn 5-45%, Al 2-14%, C 0.05-1.5% were melted and cast, then quenched from 1150°C and aged at 400-700°C. The 1150°C phase diagram with ~1% C shows γ and γ+α regions; carbon shifts phase balance. Mechanical properties for G30Yu9 and 10%Al alloys (Table 1) indicate optimal aging at 475-500°C. Microstructural evolution after 600°C aging for 10-30% Mn alloys includes γ decomposition, retained austenite, carbides, acicular austenite, and a brittle Mn-type phase at 30% Mn. Hardness of 25%Mn alloy rises from HRB 86-88 to HRC 35-37. Fig.3 shows aging temperature effects on tensile properties of 27.5Mn-9.1Al-0.92C alloy, peaking at 550°C then declining due to coarsening. Permeability increases with Al>10% after aging. Oxidation and density data underscore Al's dominant role. Tables 1-2, Figs. 1-3 are referenced.
processing_route: Melting in induction furnace in He after evacuation to 10^-3 mm Hg; also 30-kg commercial furnace
Investment casting to 65 mm × 11 mm diam. samples
microstructure: Fe-Mn-Al phase diagram at 1150°C: single-phase γ below line ab, γ+α above
Carbon stabilizes γ: α→γ transition with increasing C in 30Mn-10Al
Intense precipitation of dispersed phases at 550-600°C; negligible at 400-500°C
γ decomposes to α+carbides in 10Mn-10Al-1C alloy aged 600°C
Acicular austenite around ferrite in 20Mn alloy (Fig.2b,II)
Brittle Mn-type phase at 30Mn mainly in grain boundaries
Dispersed grain-boundary phase in 25Mn alloy; higher etchability (Fig.2b)
properties: G30Yu9 quenched: σb=84, σ0.2=57 kg/mm², δ=72%, ψ=70% (Table 1)
~9%Al alloy tensile peak at 550°C, drop above due to coarsening (Fig.3)
Permeability: nonmagnetic austenite as-quenched; increase after aging >10%Al (Table 2)
mechanisms: Coarsening of dispersed phase causing strength drop above 550°C
Fe-Al carbide precipitation increasing permeability after aging
Brittle Mn-type phase formation at high Mn

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: This chunk introduces Fermanal Fe-Mn-Al alloys, the research aim, and experimental procedures. Over 100 heats with Mn 5-45%, Al 2-14%, C 0.05-1.5% were melted and cast, then quenched from 1150°C and aged at 400-700°C. The 1150°C phase diagram with ~1% C shows γ and γ+α regions; carbon shifts phase balance. Mechanical properties for G30Yu9 and 10%Al alloys (Table 1) indicate optimal aging at 475-500°C. Microstructural evolution after 600°C aging for 10-30% Mn alloys includes γ decomposition, retained austenite, carbides, acicular austenite, and a brittle Mn-type phase at 30% Mn. Hardness of 25%Mn alloy rises from HRB 86-88 to HRC 35-37. Fig.3 shows aging temperature effects on tensile properties of 27.5Mn-9.1Al-0.92C alloy, peaking at 550°C then declining due to coarsening. Permeability increases with Al>10% after aging. Oxidation and density data underscore Al's dominant role. Tables 1-2, Figs. 1-3 are referenced.
Material systems: Fe-Mn-Al austenitic alloys (Fermanal); G30Yu9; 26.1Mn-10.2Al-1.02C; 27.5Mn-9.1Al-0.92C; 30Mn-8Al; alloys with 10% Al; steel 1Kh17N2; boiler steel 20KhM
Sample aliases: G30Yu9; alloy with 26.1% Mn, 10.2% Al, 1.02% C; alloy with 27.5% Mn, 9.1% Al, 0.92% C; alloys with 10% Al; alloy with 30% Mn, 8% Al
Processing route: Melting in induction furnace in He after evacuation to 10^-3 mm Hg; also 30-kg commercial furnace; Investment casting to 65 mm × 11 mm diam. samples; Quenching: 1150°C, 1.5 h, water quench; Aging: 400, 500, 550, 600, 700°C for 30 min to 100 h
Key variables: Mn content (5-45%); Al content (2-14%); C content (0.05-1.5%); Aging temperature; Aging time; Density vs Al; Permeability vs Al and aging; Oxidation weight gain
Microstructure: Fe-Mn-Al phase diagram at 1150°C: single-phase γ below line ab, γ+α above; Carbon stabilizes γ: α→γ transition with increasing C in 30Mn-10Al; Intense precipitation of dispersed phases at 550-600°C; negligible at 400-500°C; γ decomposes to α+carbides in 10Mn-10Al-1C alloy aged 600°C; Retained austenite increases with Mn (15→25%); Acicular austenite around ferrite in 20Mn alloy (Fig.2b,II); Brittle Mn-type phase at 30Mn mainly in grain boundaries; Dispersed grain-boundary phase in 25Mn alloy; higher etchability (Fig.2b)
Properties: G30Yu9 quenched: σb=84, σ0.2=57 kg/mm², δ=72%, ψ=70% (Table 1); Optimal aging ~10%Al: 475-500°C; ductility loss at 550-575°C; ~9%Al alloy tensile peak at 550°C, drop above due to coarsening (Fig.3); 12-14%Al alloys low ductility throughout; Permeability: nonmagnetic austenite as-quenched; increase after aging >10%Al (Table 2); Oxidation >8%Al: 6-12.5 g/m² vs 1Kh17N2 6.2, 20KhM 118 g/m² at 700°C 500h; Density: 7.24→6.68 g/cm³ (Al 6→10%); 9%Al 25-30Mn 6.75-6.85 g/cm³; 25Mn alloy hardness: HRB 86-88 → HRC 35-37
Mechanisms: Precipitation hardening from dispersed phases; Coarsening of dispersed phase causing strength drop above 550°C; Fe-Al carbide precipitation increasing permeability after aging; Brittle Mn-type phase formation at high Mn
Figures or tables: Table 1; Table 2; Fig.1; Fig.2; Fig.3

context_update.summary: This chunk reports density measurement by hydrostatic suspension for Fe-Mn-Al alloys. Density decreases from 7.24 to 6.68 g/cm³ as Al content increases from 6 to 10%. Alloys with 9% Al and 25-30% Mn have a density range of 6.75-6.85 g/cm³. These data quantify the density reduction achieved by increasing Al, relevant for lightweight design. No microstructural or processing details are introduced here. Subsequent chunks may reference these density values when discussing alloy optimization or comparing with mechanical properties.
